one_line_takeaway: A survey paper proposing the HAE (Hierarchical Autonomy Evolution) taxonomy -- Cognitive (L1) -> Executional (L2) -> Collective (L3) autonomy -- to organize AI-agent security literature and argue that collective/multi-agent-level defenses lag far behind single-agent defenses; it introduces no new attacks, defenses, or experiments of its own.
key_finding: The paper's stated finding is structural/conceptual rather than empirical: existing agent-security taxonomies (lifecycle-, trustworthy-attribute-, component-, and single-agent-autonomy-based) each miss the qualitative transitions that occur as agent autonomy escalates, and in particular under-address L3 collective/multi-agent risk as a distinct category with its own emergent social dynamics (e.g. group polarization, economic-system collapse, malicious collusion, self-replicating 'viral' prompt infection across A2A networks) rather than a simple extension of single-agent risk. The paper asserts, without new experiments, that L3 defenses (inter-agent protocol authentication, robust consensus algorithms, containment of cascading/viral failures) are markedly less mature than L1 (input filtering, adversarial training, alignment) and L2 (tool sandboxing, least-privilege access control, action-level provenance tracing) defenses, and frames this asymmetry as the paper's key research-gap contribution.
core_contribution: This is a survey/taxonomy (SoK-style) paper, not an empirical study. Its own contribution is the 'Hierarchical Autonomy Evolution' (HAE) framework, which organizes AI-agent security into three autonomy tiers -- L1 Cognitive Autonomy ('The Thinker': perception, reasoning/LLM core, memory), L2 Executional Autonomy ('The Doer': action controller, tool/API interface, digital/physical environment interaction), and L3 Collective Autonomy ('The Society': multi-agent A2A networks, role allocation, emergent social dynamics). The authors argue existing agent-security taxonomies (lifecycle-based, trustworthy-attribute-based, component-based, and Su et al.'s L1-L5 autonomy-level scheme) each fail to capture how the SAME underlying failure (e.g. hallucination) qualitatively changes character as agent autonomy increases -- from an L1 informational fallacy, to an L2 erroneous real-world operation, to L3 mass-scale misinformation propagation. Built on this framework the paper (a) presents an autonomy-aware taxonomy of threats per tier (L1: Indirect Prompt Injection, Cognitive Hijacking, Memory Corruption; L2: Confused Deputy, Tool Abuse, Environmental Damage, Unsafe Action Chains; L3: Malicious Collusion, Viral Infection, Systemic Collapse), (b) reviews defense mechanisms and evaluation protocols per tier drawn from the cited literature, and (c) introduces a 4-level 'HAE Impact Scale' (Cognitive Bypass/transient -> State Corruption/persistent -> Reality Breach/kinetic -> Systemic Cascade/contagious) used to classify ~35 cited works in a comparison table (Table 2). The paper's central claimed contribution is explicitly the identification of a 'Collective Autonomy Defense Gap': L3 multi-agent-ecosystem defenses (protocol authentication, consensus robustness, containment of self-replicating malicious instructions) are argued to be far less mature than L1/L2 defenses, and existing frameworks (notably Su et al.'s autonomy-level taxonomy) treat multi-agent systems merely as an extension of single-agent risk rather than as a distinct evolutionary stage with its own systemic/social dynamics.
threat_model: Not a single threat model -- the paper is an organizing taxonomy spanning many threat models drawn from cited work, structured by which autonomy tier the vulnerability originates in versus where its impact manifests. It explicitly distinguishes vulnerability origin from impact site (e.g., Indirect Prompt Injection is classified as an L1 threat because the failure is in the agent's perception/instruction-boundary architecture, even though its damage often only materializes during L2 tool execution). The paper also proposes a cross-layer attack-propagation chain as its own analytical device: Vertical Escalation (L1 memory/RAG poisoning corrupts the reasoning engine, which then drives L2 Tool Abuse), Horizontal Propagation (an L2 action, e.g. sending a malicious script via an email API, crosses into L3 via A2A protocols), and Systemic Amplification (the L3 network topology turns a single cognitive fault into ecosystem-wide Viral Infection/collapse). No experiments instantiate this chain; it is presented as an illustrative narrative, not a validated attack.
limitations: This is a review/position paper, so it inherits no empirical validation of its own: the HAE framework, the three-tier decomposition, the cross-layer L1->L2->L3 propagation narrative, and the 4-class HAE Impact Scale are all proposed taxonomic constructs, not tested against data or independently validated (e.g., there is no inter-rater agreement study on whether cited papers were correctly assigned to their L1/L2/L3 tier or impact class in Table 2). The paper's own comparison of HAE's 'advantages' versus four prior frameworks in Table 1 is self-assessed by the same authors proposing HAE, which is a structural limitation common to competing-taxonomy papers. Because it is a survey, all quantitative numbers cited in the review (attack success rates, latency overheads, benchmark scores) are secondhand figures pulled from other papers' reported results, carrying forward whatever methodological limitations those original studies had, without independent re-verification by this paper's authors. The paper does not report a systematic/reproducible literature search protocol (e.g., search terms, inclusion/exclusion criteria, number of papers screened) beyond stating a 'systematic analysis of literature from 2024-2025' for the four-perspective comparison.
